content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(•Jlh) ;1.!:.!-i:;(,6o • F:l\ (916) .l'.!:.!•0886 
November 21, 201 7 
Jim Wagoner 
Air Pollution Control Officer 
Butte County Air Quality Management District 
629 Entler Avenue, Suite 15 
Chico, CA 95928 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-176 
Dear Mr. Wagoner: 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Political Reform Act (the "Act"), 1 and Section 1090, not under other general conflict of interest 
prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest, including Public Contract Code. We are also 
not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice 
we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. 
In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Butte County District 
Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1 ( c )(3 ). ) We did not receive a written 
response from either entity. (Section 1097. l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for 
purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against 
any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.l(c)(5).) 
QUESTIONS 
l .  Under Section 1090 may Butte County Air District employees participate in the District's 
voucher program for changing out wood or gas stoves? 
2. Is there a conflict of interest for employee participation under the Act? 
CONCLUSIONS 
1. Under the current structure of the program administration, employees with advance 
knowledge of funding cycles for the limited fund program would have an advantage over 
the public in submitting applications and Section 1090 would bar the participation of those 
employees in the program. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FileNo. A-17-176 
Page No. 2 
2. Because we have found that the current structure of the program would create a conflict of 
interest under Section 1090, we do not address conflicts of interest under the Act. 
FACTS 
You are an Air Pollution Control Officer for the Butte County Air Quality Management 
District (''District"). Your agency periodically offers vouchers to the public to financially help 
them change out an older polluting wood stove with a new, clean-burning wood or gas stove. You 
have not allowed your staff to participate in the program because of .. self-dealing" concerns. Due 
to questions from staff, you are seeking advice as to whether District staff may participate 
following the same rules as the public. 
The voucher program provides rebate vouchers valued between $1,000 and $3,000 to 
resident homeowners in Butte County for replacement of non-EPA certified wood stoves or 
fireplace inserts with new, cleaner burning EPA certified devices. 2 The voucher is intended to 
provide an incentive and does not cover the total cost of the new cleaner burning device. The 
homeowner may use the rebate voucher with a participating retailer only, within four weeks of 
issuance. Vouchers are applied as an instant rebate off the total price of the stove. Proof of 
installation signed by the retailer, and the original sales receipt of purchase must be submitted to 
the District within thirty working days of the installation. 
You state that when a program cycle is approved, District employees' issue vouchers on a 
first come, first serve basis after reviewing the application and verifying the applicant's eligibility. 
To be eligible one must be a resident of Butte County and have an old, uncertified wood burning 
device. Applications for rebate vouchers are accepted until the funding for the program are 
depleted, and processed in the order they are received. Staffs determination of eligibility and 
issuance of a voucher does not involve discretion to pick and choose between applicants. Once the 
funding is depleted, no more vouchers are issued and staff must tum away applications. This 
happens with every funding cycle. 
While District employees participate in their official capacity in the voucher process, you 
note that it is possible to "wall off' an employee seeking a voucher from the selection process. 
However, you also note that all the District employees would be aware of the start date for the 
program ahead of the public. District employees with this knowledge would be able to start 
completing a voucher application for submittal as soon as the application notice went out to the 
public. Some of the District employees who wish to participate are designated in the agency 
conflict of interest code. 
? Qualified applicants receive a rebate voucher worth $1,000 toward a new EPA certified wood stove, or 
$1,750 towards a new pellet or natural gas stove. Special income-qualified vouchers ofup to $3,000 are available to 
those who can show proof of participation in the Low Income Heating Assistance Program, Women Infants and 
Children Supplemental Food Program, or state Medicaid at the time of application. 


ANALYSIS 
File No. A-17-176 
Page No. 3 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their 
agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended not only to strike at 
actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety. ( City of Imperial Beach v. 
Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) Under Section 1090, the prohibited act is the making of a 
contract in which the official has a financial interest. (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 
333.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) 
The prohibition applies regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all 
parties. (Id. at pp. 646-649.) 
In this matter, District employees are subject to Section 1090. Additionally, this matter 
involves a contract between the District and the applicants. Under Section 1090, transactions are 
viewed in a broad manner to avoid narrow or technical definitions of a contract. (People v. Honig, 
supra, at p. 351 citing Stigall, supra, at pp. 569, 571.) Here the District offers qualified applicants a 
rebate voucher in exchange for the applicant complying with the terms of the program. We have 
recognized in past advice letters that a rebate program, which contains an offer, acceptance and 
consideration, is a contract subject to Section 1090. (See Hentschke Advice Letter, No. A-14-187.) 
The determinative issue for your request for advice is whether all District employees are 
prohibited from participating in the program due to their knowledge of the funding cycle and 
opportunity to prepare an application prior to the public. Under Section 1090, when an employee, as 
opposed to a board member, is financially interested in a contract, the employee's agency is 
prohibited from making the contract if the employee was involved in the contract-making process. 
Generally, an employee is not financially interested in the contract if the employee plays no role 
whatsoever in the contracting process (either because such participation is outside the scope of the 
employee's duties or because the employee disqualifies himself or herself from all such 
participation). 3 
.. Participation" for purposes of Section 1090 is defined broadly and includes any act 
involving preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, reasoning, planning, drawing of 
plans and specifications, and solicitation for bids. (Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City of 
Millbrae (1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222, 237; see also Stigall, supra at p. 569.) While you state that it 
is possible to "wall off' a staff member seeking a voucher from the selection process, you have 
provided a critical fact: all District employees have enough involvement in the project that each 
employee will know that a funding cycle is approaching ahead of the public. For this reason, we 
cannot say that a District employee who submits an application "plays no role whatsoever in the 
contracting process." Therefore, Section I 090 prohibits District employees with advance 
J See 80 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 41 ( 1997) [firefighters pennitted to sell a product, which they invented in their 
private capacity, to their fire depanment so long as they did not participate in the sale in their official capacity]; 63 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 868 (1980) [real estate tax appraiser could purchase property within the county at a tax-deeded land 
sale where he did not participate in or influence the appraisal). 


File No. A-17-176 
Page No. 4 
knowledge of the funding cycle from participating in the program unless a remote or noninterest 
exception or the rule of necessity applies. 
The Legislature has created various statutory exceptions to Section 1090's prohibition 
including certain exceptions for "remote interests" and "non-interests." Of these exceptions, we 
look to the non-interest exception for the receipt of public services. Under Section 1091.5(a)(3 ), an 
officer or employee is deemed not interested in a contract if his or her interest is: 
That of a recipient of public services generally provided by the public body or 
board of which he or she is a member, on the same terms and conditions as ifhe 
or she were not a member of the body or board. (Section 1091.5(a)(3).) 
The phrase "on the same terms and conditions" requires there be no special treatment of an 
official, either express or implied, because of that person's status as an official. (Lexin v. Superior 
Courl (2010) 47 Cal.4th 1050, 1101.) Accordingly, the public services exception generally will not 
apply when the provision of the service involves an exercise of discretion by the public body that 
would allow favoritism toward officials, or occurs on terms tailored to an official's particular 
circumstances.4 
The District program as currently administered fails to meet this exception. District 
employees with knowledge of the funding cycle are not operating under the same conditions as a 
member of the public. Additionally, the District employees' discretion to extend the expiration of 
the voucher would allow favoritism toward fellow District employees. 
Due to our determination of a conflict of interest under Section 1090, we do not further 
analyze the conflict of interest issues under the Act. You may want to seek additional information if 
the District implements procedures to ensure District employees do not have knowledge of the 
funding cycle that may give them an advantage over public applicants, and to eliminate favoritism 
toward fellow employees in extending voucher dates. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Assistant General Counsel 
 
By: 
L. Karen Harrison 
Counsel, Legal Division 
LKH:jgl 
4 le.tin, supm at 1088, 1100 at note 28; 88 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. al 128 ("discretionary or highly customized 
services" benefitting official would not come within "public services" exception), 92 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. at 71.

1. Under the current structure of the program administration, employees with advance 
knowledge of funding cycles for the limited fund program would have an advantage over 
the public in submitting applications and Section 1090 would bar the participation of those 
employees in the program. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained inFileNo. A-17-176  
2. Because we have found that the current structure of the program would create a conflict of 
interest under Section 1090, we do not address conflicts of interest under the Act.

Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their

Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) Under Section 1090, the prohibited act is the making of a 
contract in which the official has a financial interest. (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 
333.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) 
The prohibition applies regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all 
parties. (Id. at pp. 646-649.)

whatsoever in the contracting process (either because such participation is outside the scope of the 
employee's duties or because the employee disqualifies himself or herself from all such 
participation). 3

involving preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, reasoning, planning, drawing of

knowledge of the funding cycle from participating in the program unless a remote or noninterest 
exception or the rule of necessity applies.

look to the non-interest exception for the receipt of public services. Under Section 1091.5(a)(3 ), an

board of which he or she is a member, on the same terms and conditions as ifhe 
or she were not a member of the body or board. (Section 1091.5(a)(3).)

2. Is there a conflict of interest for employee participation under the Act?

CONCLUSION: 1. Under the current structure of the program administration, employees with advance 
knowledge of funding cycles for the limited fund program would have an advantage over 
the public in submitting applications and Section 1090 would bar the participation of those 
employees in the program. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained inFileNo. A-17-176  
2. Because we have found that the current structure of the program would create a conflict of 
interest under Section 1090, we do not address conflicts of interest under the Act.
